headline: The Hormuz truce collapses overnight — Iran shuts the world's oil gate 'until further notice,' fires on a container ship and strikes five Gulf states while the US pounds 140 Iranian targets — and India wakes on a calm Friday close that markets will have to reprice on Monday.
the_big_picture: Yesterday the war was turning into a negotiation; today it turned back into a war — and that whiplash is the whole story. Overnight, Iran's Revolutionary Guard fired a warning shot that hit a Cyprus-flagged container ship, declared the Strait of Hormuz — the sea gate that carries a fifth of the world's oil — closed 'until the end of US interference,' and launched retaliatory strikes on US bases in Qatar, Jordan, Kuwait, Oman and the UAE. The US answered with a third night of strikes, hitting about 140 Iranian military targets (300+ over three nights). Iran's new, long-unseen Supreme Leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, finally surfaced — not to de-escalate but to call revenge for his father's killing 'inevitable.' The cruel timing for the reader: world stock and oil markets closed on Friday in a calm, hopeful mood (US crude near $71, Wall Street's fear gauge at a sleepy 15, India's Sensex up 1%), before any of this happened. So Monday opens with prices that have to catch up to a reality that has darkened over a weekend when no one could trade. Around that spine, the day rhymes with fragility close to home: a Hormuz shutdown lands straight on India's cooking-gas and petrol bills, a Pune building collapse ends an 83-hour rescue with nine dead, and Vietnam has ordered a probe into the boat capsize that killed 15 Indian tourists. The mood: a weekend of escalation the market hasn't been allowed to price — yet.

stories.title: Hormuz reignites — Iran shuts the strait and strikes five Gulf states, the US hits 140 targets, and the truce is dead
key_points: Iran fires on a Cyprus-flagged ship, declares Hormuz closed 'until further notice'
US strikes ~140 Iranian targets (300+ over three nights)
Iran retaliates against US bases in Qatar, Jordan, Kuwait, Oman; UAE, Bahrain hit
New Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei calls revenge 'inevitable'
stories.background: The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow sea lane between Iran and Oman through which roughly a fifth of the world's seaborne oil and much of its gas must pass — there is no easy detour. This year's US-Israel war with Iran, then a fragile truce, then a shooting war over shipping had whipsawed the strait for weeks. As recently as yesterday the fighting had 'paused' and turned diplomatic — US officials said Iran had privately called a tanker attack 'a mistake,' and Iran's foreign minister flew to Oman to arrange safe passage. That off-ramp has now collapsed.
stories.what_happened: Overnight into Sunday, Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) said it fired a warning shot that struck a Cyprus-flagged container ship it accused of using an 'unauthorised route,' and declared the Strait of Hormuz closed 'until the end of US interference in this region.' In retaliation for renewed US attacks, the Guards said they destroyed a command centre and drone hangars at a base in Jordan, hit a US radar site in Kuwait, struck carrier-support and refuelling platforms in Oman, and destroyed a jet-maintenance and command facility in Qatar; the UAE said its air defences engaged Iranian missiles and drones, sirens sounded in Bahrain and explosions were heard in Doha. The US military said it struck about 140 Iranian military targets — part of more than 300 over three nights — to 'degrade Iran's ability to attack civilian mariners.' And Mojtaba Khamenei, named Supreme Leader in March but unseen in public since, broke his silence to say vengeance for his father's US-Israeli killing was 'inevitable.'
stories.why_it_matters: This is the scenario markets had stopped pricing. A verbal, unsigned truce has snapped into a multi-front regional war, and Iran has done the one thing that actually threatens oil supply — formally closing Hormuz — rather than merely rattling ships. When the feared cutoff moves from 'possible' to 'declared,' the 'war premium' (the extra buyers pay to insure against lost barrels) can inflate fast. And because global markets closed Friday calm, the repricing is stored up for Monday's open rather than already reflected.
stories.ripple_effects: Expect oil to gap higher on Monday, dragging up import-heavy currencies' pain (the rupee, which closed Friday at ~95.4, is exposed), lifting safe-havens (gold, the dollar, bonds) and Wall Street's VIX 'fear gauge,' and pressuring equities — especially in oil-importing Asia. Airlines, paints, tyres, logistics and chemicals face higher input costs; energy producers and defence names get a bid. Strikes on Qatar (a top LNG exporter) and Gulf air defences add gas-supply and insurance-cost risk. The asymmetry is stark: an actual strait closure can cut supply in a way that no amount of 'tension' does.
stories.why_now: Iran chose to escalate rather than sign the public 'strait is open' pledge Washington wanted, using a disputed ship incident as the trigger; the new leadership's first public words — a revenge vow, not a climb-down — signal a hardline turn at the top.
stories.watch_next: Monday's oil open (whether Brent/WTI blow past $80), whether Hormuz traffic actually halts or Iran mines the lane, and any move toward mediation by Oman/Qatar.

key_terms.definition: A narrow sea lane between Iran and Oman carrying about a fifth of the world's seaborne oil and much of its gas; there is no easy way around it.
The extra amount buyers pay for oil to insure against conflict cutting supply — it inflates fast when a feared cutoff becomes real, as a declared strait closure now makes it.
Iran's elite military force, separate from its regular army, which controls the Gulf naval operations and much of the missile programme.

stories.the_lesson: A truce made only of words can unravel in a single night — and markets that closed pricing peace can be badly wrong-footed by a weekend of war, because prices only move when trading reopens, not when the news breaks. What actually moves oil is a real threat to barrels, and a declared chokepoint closure is exactly that.
stories.market_link: Oil, the rupee, gold, the VIX and Asian equities all face a Monday repricing: crude likely gaps up from Friday's ~$71 close, pressuring Indian and oil-importer markets while lifting energy, defence and safe-havens; a real Hormuz closure is the single biggest cross-asset risk.

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported the war cooling into a war of words — the US said Iran privately called its tanker attack 'a mistake,' Iran's foreign minister flew to Oman to arrange safe passage, the fighting had 'paused,' and oil slid toward $71 as India rallied a second day.
thread.changed: The off-ramp collapsed overnight: Iran fired on a container ship, declared Hormuz closed 'until further notice,' and struck US bases in Qatar, Jordan, Kuwait, Oman and the UAE, while the US hit ~140 Iranian targets and new Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei vowed 'inevitable' revenge — a full re-escalation markets closed Friday before pricing.

stories.title: India's weekend bind: a firm Friday close and returning foreigners — now facing a fresh oil shock on Monday
key_points: Sensex closed Friday +828 pts (+1.08%) at 77,569; Nifty +1.02% at 24,207
But for the week both slipped ~0.25% — the rally didn't fully recover
FPIs turned net buyers in July: ~₹15,157 crore inflow, ending 4 months of selling
Weekend Hormuz re-escalation now threatens Monday's open
stories.background: India imports about 85% of its oil, so the Strait of Hormuz is the single supply line its markets fear most. After the Sensex's worst day in months on 8 July, a TCS earnings beat and cooling oil powered a two-day bounce into Friday's close. A parallel worry had been foreign money leaving — but the July flow data just turned that story on its head, even as the war reignited over a weekend when no one could trade.
stories.what_happened: The Sensex ended Friday up 828 points (1.08%) at 77,569.39 and the Nifty 50 up 1.02% at 24,206.90 — yet for the full week both indices still slipped about 0.25%, showing the rally only clawed back part of the earlier slump. Crucially, foreign portfolio investors (FPIs) reversed a four-month selling streak, pouring roughly ₹15,157 crore into Indian equities in July as the rupee steadied and global risk fears eased — a genuine shift from the outflows we flagged yesterday. Brokerage previews for the coming week name the US-Iran war and Q1 FY27 earnings as the top triggers. All of that upbeat set-up now collides with the weekend's Hormuz shutdown, which markets will only get to price from Monday.
stories.why_it_matters: It captures how sentiment and reality can fall out of sync. Domestic savers (via record monthly SIP mutual-fund flows) plus newly-returning foreigners had rebuilt confidence — but a fresh oil shock arriving when markets are shut means the good mood is untested against the new bad news. Whether the FPI return survives a $80+ oil scare is the key question for the index and the rupee.
stories.ripple_effects: A Monday oil gap would pressure the rupee (fewer dollars, bigger import bill), hit rate-sensitive banks, autos and real estate, and test whether foreign buyers keep coming or flee again. Oil-marketing companies, paints, tyres and airlines are directly exposed; upstream ONGC and gas names could benefit. Q1 earnings from IT and banks land into this uncertainty.
stories.why_now: July's fund-flow data crystallised the FPI turnaround just as the Hormuz truce collapsed — pairing India's best structural news in weeks with its worst external risk on the same weekend.
stories.watch_next: Monday's Nifty open and whether it holds 24,000, the rupee versus 95.5, crude's level, and whether FPIs stay net buyers through the escalation.

key_terms.definition: Money foreign investors move into or out of a country's stocks and bonds; a swing from selling to buying is a strong confidence signal, but can reverse on a shock.
A systematic investment plan — a fixed monthly sum Indian savers put into mutual funds; record SIPs give the market a steady home-grown bid.
A single up day can still leave a market lower for the week — the weekly change shows whether a bounce actually recovered the earlier fall.

stories.the_lesson: News that breaks while markets are closed doesn't disappear — it stores up. A calm Friday close ahead of a turbulent weekend tells you almost nothing about Monday; the gap between the last price and the new reality is exactly where sharp opening moves come from.
stories.market_link: Sets up a high-stakes Monday for the Nifty, Sensex, Bank Nifty and the rupee: the FPI return is a tailwind, but a weekend oil shock from Hormuz is a fresh headwind the market hasn't yet priced.

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported India's second straight relief rally (Sensex +827) as domestically driven, noting foreigners had pulled ~60% of their India inflows since 2024 and ~$9bn in 2026 while chasing AI and US stocks.
thread.changed: The flow picture flipped: July data shows FPIs turned net buyers (~₹15,157 crore), ending four months of selling — but the two-day rally still left the week ~0.25% lower, and the weekend Hormuz re-escalation now threatens to undo the improving mood at Monday's open.

stories.title: The shock lands at home: Hormuz shutdown hits India's fuel bill as inflation tops the RBI's 4% and a monsoon warning stacks up
key_points: Hormuz closure directly threatens India's LPG and petrol/diesel prices
June retail inflation likely ~4.2% — first breach of the RBI's 4% target under new series
HDFC Bank MD warns El Niño, below-normal monsoon threaten FY27 growth and prices
India imports ~85% of its crude — the most exposed major economy
stories.background: For India, the Strait of Hormuz isn't an abstraction — it's the lane its oil and cooking gas travel through. A closure widens the import bill, weakens the rupee and feeds inflation, which the Consumer Price Index (CPI) measures as the prices households actually pay. The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) tries to keep CPI near 4%. Even before this weekend's shutdown, the slow bill from the oil scare was already showing up in the data.
stories.what_happened: With Iran declaring Hormuz closed, Indian outlets were already tracking the direct consumer hit — the price of commercial and domestic LPG cooking-gas cylinders in Delhi, Mumbai and other cities — as the clearest way the far-off war reaches a household budget. Meanwhile a Mint economists' poll pegs June retail inflation at about 4.2%, above the RBI's 4% medium-term target for the first time under the revised CPI series, driven by fuel pass-through and food costs. And HDFC Bank managing director Sashidhar Jagdishan warned that El Niño and a below-normal monsoon pose fresh risks to FY27 growth and inflation, urging 'continued vigilance' against geopolitical and trade-fragmentation risks.
stories.why_it_matters: This is the structural cost that outlasts any one-day market move — and the Hormuz shutdown threatens to reload it. Higher inflation ties the RBI's hands, leaving less room to cut interest rates to support growth. A weak or badly-distributed monsoon would pile food inflation on top of fuel inflation. Together they squeeze the household budgets and rural demand that drive India's economy.
stories.ripple_effects: Costlier fuel lifts transport, farming and manufacturing costs across the board; sticky inflation and a cautious RBI weigh on banks, autos and real estate; a wider import bill pressures the rupee and the current-account deficit (the gap when a country imports more than it exports). A monsoon miss would hit two-wheelers, tractors and consumer goods. If oil somehow settles quickly, much of this pressure eases — but the strait closure points the other way.
stories.why_now: The Hormuz shutdown lands just as June CPI is due and a top banker has flagged monsoon risk — stacking a fresh energy shock on top of inflation, weather and trade worries already in the data.
stories.watch_next: The official June CPI print, LPG and petrol/diesel price revisions if oil spikes, monsoon distribution through July–August, and the RBI's tone at its next policy meeting.

key_terms.definition: The Consumer Price Index measures how fast the prices households pay are rising; food and fuel are its biggest movers in India.
Liquefied petroleum gas — the cooking-gas cylinders most Indian households use; its price tracks global oil and gas, so a supply scare feeds straight into kitchens.
A periodic warming of the Pacific Ocean that can weaken India's monsoon rains, hurting crops and pushing up food prices.

stories.the_lesson: A distant war reaches an ordinary household through a chain of links — shipping lane to crude price to the import bill to the cooking-gas cylinder — which is why an oil chokepoint half a world away can raise the cost of dinner in Delhi.
stories.market_link: Shapes the RBI's rate path, the rupee and rate-sensitive Indian sectors (banks, autos, real estate); a sustained Hormuz closure would widen the oil-marketing companies' losses and lift upstream and gas names.

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported the 'quiet bill' broadening — June inflation set to breach the RBI's 4% ceiling, the ADB cutting FY27 growth to 6.6%, an HDFC Bank El Niño/monsoon warning, and India fighting a proposed 12.5% US tariff.
thread.changed: The bill is no longer quiet: with Hormuz declared closed over the weekend, the fuel-price channel (LPG, petrol/diesel) is now a live, direct threat rather than a background risk, on top of the inflation, monsoon and growth concerns already flagged.

stories.title: Pune building collapse: nine dead as an 83-hour rescue at a garbage depot ends
key_points: Death toll rises to 9 in a Pune building/garbage-depot collapse
83-hour rescue operation formally ends
Five people escaped safely immediately after the collapse
Latest in a monsoon season of deadly structural failures
stories.background: India's monsoon (June–September) waterlogs soil and undermines poorly-built or badly-sited structures, and every year the rains expose the cost of weak construction and lax enforcement. This collapse near a garbage depot in Pune — a major Maharashtra city — fits a grim seasonal pattern of building failures during heavy rain.
stories.what_happened: The death toll in a building collapse in Pune rose to nine, with authorities ending an 83-hour rescue operation, according to live reporting; one person had been missing under a garbage dump as crews searched the rubble. Five people managed to get out safely immediately after the structure came down. The prolonged, multi-day effort underscores how hard rescue becomes when debris and waste are involved.
stories.why_it_matters: Beyond the lives lost, repeated monsoon-season collapses point to systemic gaps — unsafe construction, weak enforcement of building codes, and vulnerable siting near hazards like waste dumps. For a rapidly urbanising country, building safety and emergency response are a recurring test of local governance.
stories.ripple_effects: Expect scrutiny of construction approvals and structural audits in Pune and beyond, compensation for victims' families, and renewed debate over enforcement. Such events also strain municipal disaster-response capacity during an already demanding monsoon.
stories.why_now: The collapse happened days ago; the story peaked today as the long rescue formally concluded and the toll was confirmed at nine amid an active monsoon.
stories.watch_next: The final casualty count, any accountability action on the building's approval and safety, and further rain-driven collapses as the monsoon continues.

key_terms.definition: India's June–September rainy season; the heavy rain both waters crops and exposes weak infrastructure, causing floods and collapses.

stories.the_lesson: Disasters like building collapses are rarely just 'accidents' — they sit at the intersection of weather, construction quality and enforcement, so the same heavy rain hits some communities far harder than others depending on how well the rules were followed.

stories.title: America's job engine cools — payrolls up just 57,000 and the labour force shrinks as the Fed splits on inflation
key_points: US added only 57,000 jobs in June vs ~115,000 expected
Unemployment fell to 4.2% — but for the 'wrong reasons' as people quit looking
Labour-force participation hit its lowest in 50 years (ex-Covid)
Fed officials divided on inflation; US home prices hit an all-time high
stories.background: The US monthly jobs report is the world's most-watched economic gauge, because a cooling American labour market shapes what the Federal Reserve (the US central bank) does with interest rates — and US rates ripple into every market, including India's. A slowing jobs market usually argues for rate cuts; sticky inflation argues against them.
stories.what_happened: US nonfarm payrolls rose just 57,000 in June — roughly half the ~115,000 expected — and prior months' figures pointed to a clear slowdown. The unemployment rate ticked down to 4.2%, but analysts flagged it as misleading: it fell because discouraged workers stopped looking for jobs, pushing the labour-force participation rate to its lowest in 50 years outside the Covid shock. Private payrolls (ADP) had already come in soft at 98,000. Minutes from the Fed's June meeting and fresh commentary showed officials divided on whether inflation is beaten, even as US home prices hit a record high — a reminder that parts of the economy remain hot.
stories.why_it_matters: A weakening jobs market with a shrinking workforce is a murkier signal than a clean slowdown — it can mean demand is softening (dovish, pro-rate-cut) or that supply of workers is drying up (which can keep wage inflation sticky). With the Fed itself split and housing still hot, the path of US rates — and the dollar — is genuinely uncertain, and that uncertainty now collides with a fresh oil-price shock from Hormuz.
stories.ripple_effects: Softer jobs data nudges the Fed toward cuts, which typically weakens the dollar and supports emerging-market flows into places like India — a tailwind for the rupee and Indian equities. But if the new oil spike reignites inflation, the Fed may hold, keeping the dollar firm and pressuring the rupee. Rate-sensitive US tech and housing swing on every data point.
stories.why_now: June's jobs report and the Fed's June minutes have just landed, sharpening the rates debate exactly as a Middle East oil shock threatens to complicate the inflation picture.
stories.watch_next: The next US CPI inflation print, Fed officials' signals on a first cut, and whether the oil spike changes the rate-cut math.

key_terms.definition: The monthly count of US jobs added (excluding farms); the headline gauge of how strong the US economy is.
The share of working-age people either working or looking for work; when it falls, a lower jobless rate can mask people giving up rather than finding jobs.
The US Federal Reserve, which sets US interest rates; its decisions move the dollar and ripple into markets worldwide, India included.

stories.the_lesson: A falling unemployment rate isn't always good news — it matters why it fell. If people are leaving the workforce rather than finding jobs, the headline number can hide a weakening economy, which is why analysts read the details, not just the top line.
stories.market_link: US rate expectations drive the dollar and global risk appetite; a dovish Fed helps the rupee and Indian equities, but a Hormuz-driven oil spike could force the Fed to stay cautious — the two forces now pull against each other.

stories.title: Typhoon Bavi makes landfall in eastern China — up to 2 million evacuated in a second storm in a week
key_points: Typhoon Bavi makes landfall in China's Zhejiang province
Up to ~2 million people evacuated around Wenzhou
China's second typhoon in a week; among the strongest in decades
Days of heavy rain and flooding forecast
stories.background: The western Pacific typhoon season is at its peak, and typhoons are the same storms called hurricanes in the Atlantic. Bavi is unusually large — roughly 1,000km across — and its track brushed chip-making Taiwan before turning on China's densely populated, factory-dense coast. It is China's second typhoon in a single week.
stories.what_happened: Typhoon Bavi made landfall in eastern China's Zhejiang province, with authorities evacuating up to about two million people, the city of Wenzhou close to the storm's path. Forecasters warned of destructive winds and days of heavy rain and flooding. The storm had earlier tracked across Taiwan and, in its wider path, triggered landslides that killed people in the Philippines — shaping up as one of the strongest storms in decades.
stories.why_it_matters: A giant typhoon over China's manufacturing coast and near Taiwan is an economic risk, not just a weather one. Major storms can halt ports, factories and — near Taiwan — the chip fabs that sit at the heart of global electronics and AI-hardware supply chains. Mass evacuations on this scale also test emergency systems and insurance.
stories.ripple_effects: Evacuations and shutdowns disrupt manufacturing and shipping; any hit to Taiwan's fabs or China's export ports ripples into global electronics and AI-hardware supply. Rebuilding and relief costs follow, and back-to-back storms raise resilience and insurance spending across the region.
stories.why_now: Peak typhoon season has produced two storms in a week, and Bavi's exceptional size forced mass evacuations as it struck China's coast this weekend.
stories.watch_next: Bavi's inland track and flooding, and whether it disrupts Taiwan's chip fabs or China's coastal ports and industry.

key_terms.definition: A powerful tropical storm in the western Pacific — the same phenomenon called a hurricane in the Atlantic.
A semiconductor fabrication plant; Taiwan hosts the world's most advanced ones, so any disruption ripples through global electronics.

stories.the_lesson: Climate risk is economic risk — a storm's path over a manufacturing hub can matter as much to global supply chains as any boardroom decision, which is why markets now watch the forecast near places like Taiwan and China's coast.
stories.market_link: A chip-supply watch item: a major hit to Taiwan's fabs or China's coastal industry would ripple into global electronics and AI-hardware supply.

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported Bavi nearing landfall in Zhejiang after crossing Taiwan, with hundreds of thousands evacuated around Wenzhou and 15 killed earlier in Philippine landslides.
thread.changed: Bavi has now made landfall in eastern China, with evacuations scaled up to as many as two million people and days of heavy rain and flooding forecast — its second landfall region in a week of back-to-back storms.

stories.title: China weighs curbing overseas access to its AI models — a new front in the tech cold war
key_points: China mulls curbs on foreign access to top AI models
Alibaba, ByteDance and Z.ai reportedly called into Commerce Ministry talks
Would hit US firms leaning on cheap Chinese models (Qwen, DeepSeek, GLM)
Existing open weights stay; future ones may be restricted
stories.background: For two years Chinese labs have released powerful, cheap, 'open-weight' AI models — versions whose underlying files anyone can download and run — that many US companies quietly adopted to cut soaring AI costs. That made Chinese models a backdoor into the global AI economy. Now Beijing is reportedly weighing whether to keep that door open, mirroring US curbs on selling advanced chips to China.
stories.what_happened: China is considering restricting overseas access to its most advanced AI models, according to a Reuters report, with Alibaba, ByteDance and Z.ai summoned to talks at the Ministry of Commerce. The move would hit US firms — names like Airbnb, Cursor and DoorDash were cited — that leaned on inexpensive Chinese models such as Qwen, DeepSeek and GLM-5.2 to cut costs. Existing open weights would reportedly stay available, but future, more capable models might not be released freely.
stories.why_it_matters: It signals the US-China tech rivalry spreading from hardware (chips) to the AI 'brains' themselves. If China restricts its best models, US firms that had offloaded costs onto cheap Chinese AI would face higher bills or a scramble for alternatives — and the world could split into rival AI ecosystems, much as it has for chips and telecom gear.
stories.ripple_effects: Higher AI costs for firms that relied on Chinese models; a potential boost for Western model-makers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google) and for the whole 'AI is expensive' narrative that supports chip and data-centre demand. For India's IT and startup scene, which uses a mix of models, it's a reminder that AI supply, like oil, can be weaponised.
stories.why_now: As Chinese models closed the quality gap with US ones and foreign adoption grew, Beijing gained leverage — and is reportedly weighing whether to use it, echoing Washington's own export controls.
stories.watch_next: Whether the curbs are formalised, which models are affected, and how US firms and Western AI labs respond.

key_terms.definition: AI models whose core files are released publicly so anyone can download, run and build on them — the opposite of a closed model you can only use via a company's service.
Government limits on selling sensitive technology abroad; the US restricts advanced chips to China, and China could now restrict its AI models in return.

stories.the_lesson: Any input a country dominates can become a lever of power — first it was chips and oil, now it may be AI models. When one side controls something the other needs, access itself becomes a bargaining chip.
stories.market_link: Potentially supportive for Western AI-model makers and the chip/data-centre complex if firms must pay up for AI; a background risk for companies that built on cheap Chinese models.

stories.title: Vietnam orders a probe into the Phu Quoc boat capsize that killed 15 Indian tourists
key_points: Vietnam orders an investigation into the speedboat capsize
15 Indian tourists killed (13 men, 2 women); 21 rescued, some critical
PM Modi condoled; Indian embassy set up control rooms for families
Boat overturned in strong winds ~400m off Phu Quoc island
stories.background: Phu Quoc, a resort island off southern Vietnam, has become popular with Indian tourists as outbound travel booms with rising incomes. Speedboat transfers between islands are routine — and dangerous when the sea turns. A boat carrying 32 Indian tourists and four crew capsized on Saturday, killing 15.
stories.what_happened: Vietnam has ordered a probe into the capsize, which killed 15 Indian tourists — 13 men and two women — with 21 people rescued, some in critical condition, after the speedboat overturned about 400 metres from Phu Quoc in strong winds and high waves. Prime Minister Modi said he was 'extremely saddened,' and India's embassy and consulate set up control rooms and are coordinating with Vietnamese authorities on identification and support for families.
stories.why_it_matters: Beyond the human loss, this tests India's consular machinery — how fast a government can identify victims, support grieving families and coordinate with a foreign state. As more Indians travel abroad, tourist safety and rapid embassy response are becoming part of what a modern government is judged on. A formal Vietnamese probe may also reshape boat-safety rules for tourists.
stories.ripple_effects: Expect scrutiny of Vietnamese boat-safety standards and Indian tour operators, possible advisories on weather-dependent transfers, and continued grief across the affected states as identification and repatriation proceed.
stories.why_now: The accident happened Saturday; today Vietnam formally ordered an investigation as rescue and identification continued, keeping it a live, developing story.
stories.watch_next: The probe's findings on cause and operator responsibility, the condition of the injured, and any new weather-safety rules for tourist boats.

key_terms.definition: Help a country's embassy gives its citizens abroad in a crisis — identifying victims, aiding families, repatriating remains, and liaising with local authorities.

stories.the_lesson: As a country grows richer its citizens travel more, and 'traveller safety' abroad becomes a real test of the state — the speed and competence of an embassy's response is part of what a modern government is judged on.

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported the speedboat capsizing off Phu Quoc, killing 15 Indian tourists, with Modi 'extremely saddened' and the Indian embassy setting up control rooms.
thread.changed: Vietnam has now formally ordered an investigation into the tragedy, with the toll confirmed at 15 and 21 rescued, as identification and family support continue.

stories.title: Deadly heat spans two continents — Spain's wildfire kills 12+ as a US heat dome bakes two-thirds of the country
key_points: One of Spain's deadliest wildfires kills at least 12, with 23 missing
At least four Britons among the dead near Almería
A US 'heat dome' set to push temperatures over 100°F across two-thirds of the US
UK classrooms top 40C in a second European heatwave
stories.background: Southern Europe is gripped by its second heatwave of the summer — prolonged extreme heat that dries out vegetation and turns any spark into a fast-moving wildfire — and now the US is facing its own 'heat dome,' a high-pressure lid that traps hot air over a region for days. Scientists link the rising frequency and intensity of such heat to a warming climate.
stories.what_happened: A fast-spreading wildfire in Los Gallardos, in Spain's Almería province, killed at least 12 people — including at least four Britons — with 23 unaccounted for, some victims found in burnt-out cars as they tried to flee. France's heat emergency shut the Eiffel Tower and Paris museums early, and UK classrooms topped 40C. Across the Atlantic, forecasters warned a heat dome would push temperatures above 100°F (38°C) across roughly two-thirds of the US.
stories.why_it_matters: Clustered extreme-heat events cause deaths and strain emergency services, power grids, insurance and infrastructure at the same time — and now on two continents at once. Heat cuts labour productivity, hits farming and tourism, and forces cities and grids to cope with conditions they weren't built for. It's a recurring reminder that climate risk is an economic and public-health risk.
stories.ripple_effects: Deadly heat and fire raise disaster-relief and insurance costs, dent tourism and farm output, and spike electricity demand for cooling — which can strain power grids on both sides of the Atlantic. Rebuilding and adaptation spending follows the immediate loss and disruption.
stories.why_now: Peak Northern-Hemisphere summer has delivered a second European heatwave and a sprawling US heat dome simultaneously, pushing temperatures past what infrastructure was built for.
stories.watch_next: Whether Spain's fire is contained and the missing found, and whether the US heat dome triggers grid stress or new wildfires.

key_terms.definition: A prolonged period of excessively hot weather; it dries out land and vegetation, sharply raising wildfire risk.
A high-pressure system that acts like a lid, trapping hot air over a large area for days and driving temperatures to extremes.

stories.the_lesson: A warming climate turns 'hot weather' into a systemic shock — the same pattern can kill in a wildfire, close landmarks, buckle transport and stress power grids across whole continents at once, because societies were built for a cooler normal.
stories.market_link: Heat and fire pressure agriculture, insurance, tourism and power demand in Europe and the US; extended heat lifts electricity use and can strain grids.

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported the Spanish wildfire killing 12+ with four Britons among the dead and 23 missing, France shutting the Eiffel Tower early, and UK classrooms topping 40C in a European heat emergency.
thread.changed: The heat has now jumped continents: a US 'heat dome' is forecast to push temperatures over 100°F across two-thirds of the country, even as Spain still battles the fire and Europe's heatwave persists.

stories.title: Overshadowed by the Iran war, Israel now controls nearly 70% of Gaza as hospitals go dark
key_points: Israel now controls close to 70% of Gaza, up from ~50% at the truce
Power cuts plunge Gaza hospitals into darkness amid continued strikes
A US-brokered 'ceasefire' has coincided with a deeper Israeli push
The Iran war has pulled attention away from Gaza's crisis
stories.background: The US brokered a Gaza ceasefire last year, when Israel controlled about half the territory. A ceasefire is meant to freeze fighting — but this one has coincided with Israeli forces pushing deeper rather than pulling back, as the enclave's basic systems collapse. With the world fixated on the US-Iran war, Gaza's slow-motion crisis gets less scrutiny.
stories.what_happened: Reporting finds Israel now controls nearly 70% of Gaza — up sharply from roughly half when the ceasefire began — having expanded its zone of control over nine months. An energy crisis has plunged Gaza's hospitals into darkness, with power cuts crippling facilities as Israeli attacks persist. This weekend a Palestinian aid worker who organised World Cup screenings was reported killed in an Israeli strike. The combination of expanding control and collapsing infrastructure marks a steady deterioration despite the nominal truce.
stories.why_it_matters: It shows how a 'ceasefire' can mask an ongoing shift of control and a worsening humanitarian catastrophe. Hospitals without power cannot run ventilators, incubators or operating theatres, turning power cuts into a life-and-death issue. The crisis keeps the Israeli-Palestinian conflict a live source of regional instability — now compounded by the reignited Iran war next door.
stories.ripple_effects: A deepening Gaza crisis fuels regional tension, complicates Arab-state relations with Israel, and sustains diplomatic pressure at the UN and in Western capitals — all now entangled with a Gulf war that has drawn in Qatar, the UAE and others.
stories.why_now: The nine-month mark of the ceasefire and a fresh hospital power collapse drew attention back to Gaza, even as the Iran escalation dominates Middle East headlines.
stories.watch_next: Whether power and aid reach Gaza's hospitals, and whether the Iran war spills further into the wider conflict.

key_terms.definition: An agreement to stop fighting; it can be partial or fragile, and does not always mean forces withdraw or conditions improve.
The area a military physically holds and administers — expanding it shifts facts on the ground regardless of a truce's terms.

stories.the_lesson: A ceasefire is a label, not a guarantee — what matters is the reality underneath it: who controls the ground and whether basic systems like power and hospitals still function; watch conditions, not just declarations.
stories.market_link: A background regional-risk factor now amplified by the Hormuz escalation next door, which drives the same oil and safe-haven premiums.

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported Israel controlling nearly 70% of Gaza — up from ~50% at the truce — with power cuts plunging hospitals into darkness as attacks persisted.
thread.changed: The crisis continues under the shadow of the reignited Iran war, with a Palestinian aid worker reported killed in an Israeli strike this weekend and hospitals still crippled by power cuts.

also_notable.title: Former Qatar Emir Sheikh Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani dies at 74
also_notable.why: The architect of modern Qatar and Al Jazeera dies just as his country is drawn into the Iran-US crossfire — a symbolic loss at a fraught Gulf moment.

also_notable.why: A read on the Indian consumer — steady growth from the value-retail giant even amid inflation worries — as Q1 earnings season builds.

also_notable.title: Cloudburst floods hotels and homes in J&K's Pahalgam; no casualties reported
also_notable.why: Another violent monsoon burst in the Himalayas — part of a season swinging between destructive downpours and patchy overall rain.

also_notable.title: Indian-origin NASA astronaut Anil Menon set for his first ISS mission
also_notable.why: A milestone for the Indian diaspora in space, as Menon prepares to launch to the International Space Station aboard a Soyuz.
